title: Migration Data Transformation | Stats NZ
content:
## Migration Data Transformation
14 May 2026, 10:45am
Access the latest news and reports from the Migration Data Transformation page.
14 May 2026 : See latest Comparison of provisional and final migration estimates: August 2017 to March 2026 (previously available on monthly migration release).

## About the Migration Data Transformation project
The Migration Data Transformation project ran in 2018–2019. It removed the statistical dependency on the data provided by departure cards and transformed external (international) migration data with the sustainable implementation of a new measure of migration.
We made methodological changes which enabled us to continue producing official measures of migration, tourism, and estimates of population with the removal of nearly 7 million paper-based departure cards completed by people leaving New Zealand annually. This involved making more use of existing information, such as border crossing information from Customs (passport data), arrival cards, and integrated administrative data.
Latest news and reports Additional information about the project - Impact on variables - Feedback on new tables - Changes to Infoshare - Download documents Frequently asked questions - The 12/16-month rule - Why we use the ‘12/16-month rule’ and not a different rule - How the ‘12/16-month rule’ deals with months of different length - Comparison with the Australian method - Impact on population estimates - Outcomes-based and intentions-based measures compared - Why migration estimates change - The statistical model used to provisionally estimate migration - Training data used in the model - Why net migration is not simply the difference between all arrivals and all departures - Impact on international travel statistics Contact us

### Timeline of Migration Data Transformation
Rolling training window for provisional migration estimates changed: Adjusting the training window for the international migration model
New migration series published on migrant departures to Australia: Migration to Australia halves
International migration estimates extended back to 2001
Population estimates incorporate historical outcomes migration measure back to Sep qtr 2013 (Jun qtr 2019): New Zealand’s population grows 1.6 percent in June year
First official subnational outcomes migration measure published (final estimates using IDI): Net migration remains around 50,000
Population estimates incorporate outcomes migration measure (Dec qtr 2018): National population estimated at 4.93 million
First official outcomes migration measure published; separate International Migration and International Travel releases: International migration uses new official measure Total international travellers pass 14 million

### Feedback on new tables
We welcome feedback on the Excel tables published in the International migration and International travel releases. Please send feedback to demography@stats.govt.nz .

### Changes to Infoshare
Changes to ‘International Travel and Migration’ (ITM) series occurred from 25 January 2019. This included new series, changes to existing series, and discontinuation of some series. For a summary of initial changes, and detailed Infoshare identifiers for new series, see Download documents (below).
The Tourism and migration data visualiser will remain available as an experimental tool.

## Frequently asked questions

### The 12/16-month rule
The 12/16-month rule is a way of classifying border crossings as short-term or long-term on the basis of whether travellers spend 12 months (or more) of the following 16 months in New Zealand.
- A migrant arrival is an overseas resident who arrives in New Zealand and cumulatively spends 12 out of the next 16 months in New Zealand.
- A migrant departure is a New Zealand resident who departs New Zealand and cumulatively spends 12 out of the next 16 months out of New Zealand.
A New Zealand resident is someone living in New Zealand, irrespective of their citizenship or visa status. Overseas residents and New Zealand residents both comprise New Zealand citizens and non-New Zealand citizens.
The 12/16-month rule gives a transparent and objective method of classifying travellers, rather than relying on the stated intentions of travellers on passenger cards.
Text alternative for the 12/16-month rule

### Why we use the ‘12/16-month rule’ and not a different rule
The ‘12/16-month rule’ aligns with United Nations recommendations around the measurement of migration , and Stats NZ's resident population concept as used in historical and current migration and population statistics. The ‘12/16-month rule’ is also used by the Australian Bureau of Statistics in their official measures of Australian migration.
One benefit of the ‘12/16-month rule’ is that it does not require consecutive time to be spent in New Zealand or overseas for a traveller to be counted as a migrant arrival/departure. For example, an international student who spends the term-time in New Zealand and holidays overseas, over successive years, will be counted as a migrant arrival at the time of their initial border crossing if they satisfy the 12/16-month criterion.
Another benefit of linking arrivals and departures to create travel histories is that alternative rules can be applied for different definitions. For example, migration estimates using a 6/8 or 9/12 month rule could be derived; or travellers that spend more than 6 months but less than 12 months in New Zealand could be analysed.

### How the ‘12/16-month rule’ deals with months of different length
The calculation to classify travellers as short-term or long-term is actually based on days – a 365/487 day threshold – but this is summarised for simplicity as a 12/16-month rule.

### Comparison with the Australian method
Our New Zealand migration estimation method has been developed to meet the requirements of New Zealand customers. The Australian Bureau of Statistics also implements a 12/16-month rule, but they wait about 5½ months before publishing their initial provisional estimates. They construct provisional estimates for groups of people rather than individuals. Australian migration estimates are available quarterly, not monthly.
The Australian method for provisional estimates does not meet the requirements of New Zealand customers because:
- monthly estimates are needed within 2 months of the reference period
- individual data are needed to enable integration with other administrative data (for example, IDI – Integrated Data Infrastructure ) to derive subnational statistics and to enable the utility of integrated data more generally
- individual data enables flexible aggregation to any sub-group.

### Impact on population estimates
The new outcomes-based migration estimates have been used in national population estimates released from February 2019 (see National population estimates ). National population estimates back to September quarter 2013 were revised in August 2019 (see National population estimates: At 30 June 2019 ) to incorporate interim new migration estimates. The new outcomes-based migration estimates have been used in subnational population estimates released from October 2019 (see Subnational population estimates ). Both national and subnational population estimates have been revised back to 30 June 2013 on 23 September 2020 to incorporate 2018 Census and 2018 post-enumeration survey results, and any updated final birth, death, and migration estimates.
After each census, we revise the intercensal population estimates to be consistent with the latest census-base estimated resident population, which implicitly corrects for any discrepancy in estimated population change. Therefore, revising population estimates up to 30 June 2013 is unnecessary.

### Outcomes-based and intentions-based measures compared
The previous migration measure (permanent and long-term (PLT) migration) was estimated from travellers’ statements on arrival or departure cards – based on how long they intended to stay in New Zealand (or be away). This intentions-based measure was timely; however, traveller behaviour was not always consistent with the stated intentions at their border crossing. This may be due to circumstances changing, misunderstanding the questions on the traveller cards and incorrectly reporting their intentions, or deciding to extend their visa or stay/absence.
The outcomes-based measure is estimated from the actual travel histories of people travelling in and out of New Zealand. This provides a more accurate measure of migration – a traveller is classified as a migrant based on their actual movements.
The outcomes arrival and departure estimates are consistently higher than intentions PLT estimates.
The outcomes net migration estimates are sometimes higher, and sometimes lower, than intentions PLT estimates. In 2010–2018, net migration using outcomes was lower than intentions.
Outcomes versus intentions: Measuring migration based on travel histories has more information that compares the two measures.

### Why migration estimates change
The outcomes-based measure of migration with provisional and final estimates is now the official way we measure migration in New Zealand. The results build on the outcomes-based measure of migration that was released from May 2017 and enabled the removal of the departure card.
The new approach uses passport data to link arrivals and departures and accurately measure how long people spend in, or out of, New Zealand after their initial border crossing. To classify a border crossing as a migrant movement, we need to observe up to 16 months of travel history.
With this new approach it takes 17 months before final migration estimates are available. To produce timely results, we use a statistical model to produce provisional migration estimates. Statistics produced using these provisional estimates have uncertainty for 16 months; after this time we can finalise the classification of all border crossings (according to the 12/16-month rule ).
As new data becomes available, the provisional migration model has more information about the border crossings it is trying to estimate. So, with an extra month of data available, this causes shifts in the estimated number of migrant arrivals and migrant departures, and thus changes in the net migration estimates.
For example, the extra data will indicate travellers who have now departed New Zealand, or travellers who were away who have since returned to New Zealand.
Compared with total border crossings, the number of migrants is very small. Of every 50 people crossing our border, typically 49 are short-term movements and only 1 is a migrant arriving or departing.
The migration estimates become more certain after each subsequent month. For a typical month, 1 in 4 arrivals are classified with certainty for the first estimate after six weeks (after the end of the reference month). This increases to 9 in 10 after four months. The monthly revisions can therefore be expected to become relatively small after about five months, as we can calculate the duration of stay/absence more definitively.
Of the 14 million border crossings in the December 2018 year, 81 percent of border crossings were classified with certainty at the time of the first estimate in mid-February 2019. The remaining 19 percent represent 2.6 million border crossings, so a small change in classification can affect the migration estimates.
Customers therefore have a choice of using the most timely migration estimates which have more uncertainty or waiting a few months until the migration estimates become more certain.

### The statistical model used to provisionally estimate migration
We use a unit record machine-learning model to classify travellers whose migrant status is uncertain. This is a classification model, not a forecasting model, applied to individual border crossings.
The proportion of travellers in each month who have an uncertain migrant status ranges from a majority for the most recent few months, to a minority for all other months. This is because most travellers are coming and going within a few weeks and their migrant status can be logically finalised for at least one of their border crossings.
The model learns about the features of border crossings that make them more or less likely to be migrant crossings, by looking at millions of historical records.
- Features from the border crossing, such as the direction of crossing, and date of the crossing.
- Features of the 12/16-month rule, such as the amount of time in/out of New Zealand, the amount of time that has passed since the border crossing, the count of border crossings after the crossing to be classified, whether a traveller is still in New Zealand, and their resident status before the border crossing.
- Features from the passport data, such as the age, sex, and citizenship of traveller, and the broad type of visa they hold.
It then applies what it learns to the set of border crossings that are unknown (because not enough time has passed), at an individual crossing level, to estimate the probability that a particular arrival or departure is a migrant arrival or departure. As is common practice across statistics, early estimates are provided and are later revised as more data become available.
Net migration is not modelled – it is the difference between migrant arrivals and migrant departures.
See also Fact sheet 2: An outcomes-based approach to measuring migration – method overview .

### Training data used in the model
To incorporate changes in traveller behaviour over time, a three-year rolling window of historic arrivals and departures is used to train the model. To cope with the impact of border restrictions due to COVID-19 on provisional migration estimates, a change to the training window was implemented in August 2024.
The 36-month rolling window ended with the most recent month for which migrant status had been finalised. For example, for migration estimates up to June 2023 (published in August 2023), this window was up to February 2022, where the migrant status of all travellers was finalised.
The 36-month rolling window ends with the four oldest provisional months. For example, for migration estimates up to June 2024 (published in August 2024), this window includes the 32 most recent finalised months up to February 2023 plus finalised crossings from the four oldest provisional months, March−June 2023. Adjusting the training window for the international migration model (published 6 August 2024) provides more information.
Regardless of the training window, the latest border crossings are used in the model each month. For example, the June 2024 estimates published in mid-August 2024 take account of border crossings through July 2024.
A typical 36-month training window, outside of the COVID-19 period, comprises 30−40 million border crossings. Some sampling is applied to reduce the training data to a manageable 2−3 million records for each model run.
Final migration estimates are unaffected by the training window because they do not require modelling of migrant status.

### Why net migration is not simply the difference between all arrivals and all departures
Gross arrivals and departures include tourists coming and going, business travellers, New Zealanders taking holidays, and other short-term travellers. These people are not migrants and they do not change the population living in New Zealand.
A migrant is someone changing their country of residence. A 12-month threshold is used internationally to differentiate between migrants and non-migrants.
In 2019, pre-COVID, 1 in every 55 people crossing the border was a migrant. In 2021, as inbound and outbound tourism shrank, the ratio was 1 in 7. Seasonal fluctuations of inbound and outbound tourists, often around school holidays, means the difference of all arrivals and departures is a poor measure of net migration over short periods of time.
The difference between all arrivals and all departures is interesting, as this indicates how the number of people physically in New Zealand is changing. But most population statistics relate to the resident population – the population usually living in an area – as this is most pertinent to education and health needs, the labour and housing markets, the tax base, superannuation, and so on. For these purposes it is important to measure migrant arrivals and migrant departures.
To meet different needs, however, Stats NZ will continue to publish statistics on all movements, visitors from overseas, NZ-resident travellers, and migrants (see International travel , International migration , and detailed data in Infoshare ).

### Impact on international travel statistics
The MDT project largely affected the measurement of external (international) migration in and out of New Zealand, but there was some impact on international travel statistics. For example, statistics on NZ-resident travellers (outbound travel sector) are now focused on arrivals rather than departures, as they rely on information from the arrival cards. Statistics on overseas visitors to New Zealand (inbound travel sector) are largely unaffected (see Impact on variables ).
International travel statistics are therefore still based on the stated intentions of travellers when they cross the border. For example, the classification of travellers as ‘NZ-resident travellers’ or ‘overseas visitors’ is still largely based on their responses on the arrival card. If an overseas visitor ends up staying for 12 months or more, they will be counted as a migrant arrival, but the overseas visitor statistics are not revised to reflect their eventual status. Any revisions to short-term travel statistics would generally be small relative to the large volume of short-term travellers.
This does mean that the intentions-based short-term travel numbers and the outcomes-based migration estimates are not fully additive to the total border crossings.

### Text alternative for the 12/16-month rule
The flow diagram of the 12/16-month rule shows steps in the decision process we use to determine the migrant classification of a person crossing the New Zealand border, starting with whether they are a New Zealand resident or non-resident at the time of arriving or departing. Arrivals flow: residents who arrive remain classified as residents; non-residents who spend 12+ of the next 16 months in the country are classified as migrant arrivals; non-residents who do not stay in the country for that amount of time remain classified as non-residents (not migrants). Departures flow: non-residents who leave remain classified as non-residents (not migrants); residents who spend 12+ of the next 16 months out of the country are classified as migrant departures; residents who do not stay out of the country for that amount of time remain classified as residents (not migrants).
Email demography@stats.govt.nz if you have any questions about migration data transformation, provisional and final migration estimates, or impacts on tourism and migration statistics.
Page updated : 13 December 2024
